frameworks.name: rational inattention
frameworks.one_line: optimal allocation of limited mental effort under information-processing costs.

frameworks.name: Bayesian inference with noisy mental simulation
frameworks.one_line: noisy future-value simulations combined with priors yield apparent discounting.

frameworks.name: temporal discounting (intrinsic-preference account)
frameworks.one_line: traditional view of impatience as intrinsic preference for sooner rewards.

core_question: Can the magnitude effect in intertemporal choice arise from adaptive modulation of simulation noise under rational inattention?
key_assumptions: noisy mental simulations of future rewards are combined with prior beliefs via Bayes rule
internal precision is a costly resource allocated to maximize expected utility
mental effort scales adaptively with task demands / reward magnitude
manipulations: re-analysis across two prior data sets plus one new experiment
key_findings: behavioral signatures linking choice stochasticity to the magnitude effect
magnitude effect emerges from optimal mental-effort allocation
temporal discounting patterns reproduced from internal uncertainty rather than pure preference
interpretations: some aspects of temporal discounting reflect adaptive responses to information-processing costs
intertemporal choice can be reframed as a rational-inattention computation rather than intrinsic impatience
